region_of_origin: Global (widely cultivated; major producers include USA, Canada, Russia, Australia)

allergens: gluten (oats — may contain or be cross-contaminated with gluten-containing grains)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of oat bran is approximately 40 g (about 4–5 tablespoons), which is commonly used as a hot cereal base or added to smoothies, baked goods, and yogurt.

nutrient_source.title: Oat bran, raw — USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 169722 (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 169722 'Oat bran, raw' is an exact match for uncooked oat bran. Core macros and micronutrients are drawn from this entry. Singapore HPB database values (energy 246 kcal, protein 17.3 g, fat 7.03 g, carbohydrate 66.22 g, sugar 1.45 g, sodium 4 mg, dietary fibre 15.4 g, potassium 566 mg, calcium 58 mg, iron 5.41 mg, cholesterol 0 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy and are retained as the primary values per input provenance (HPB, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrients are supplemented from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169722.

gi_source.title: Oat bran, unprocessed — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) report oat bran (unprocessed/raw) with a GI of approximately 55. According to the University of Sydney GI Database thresholds, a GI of 55 or less is classified as 'low'. Glycemic load per 40 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 26.3) / 100 ≈ 14.5. Available carbohydrate per serving = (66.22 − 15.4) × 0.40 = 20.33 g net; using total carbohydrate basis per standard GL formula: (55 × 66.22 × 0.40) / 100 = 14.6, rounded to 14.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100 g), high potassium (566 mg/100 g), and high soluble dietary fibre (15.4 g/100 g) supporting DASH diet alignment
hypertension.guidance: Oat bran is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension: its negligible sodium content (4 mg/100 g), high potassium-to-sodium ratio, and beta-glucan soluble fibre are consistent with DASH dietary principles and have been associated with modest reductions in systolic blood pressure in clinical trials; prepare without added salt or high-sodium toppings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High beta-glucan soluble fibre (15.4 g/100 g) slowing glucose absorption; low sugar (1.45 g/100 g); low GI (~55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Oat bran is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled: the high beta-glucan soluble fibre content significantly attenuates postprandial glucose response and improves insulin sensitivity, and the low GI of ~55 is substantially lower than refined grain alternatives; a standard 40 g dry serving yields a glycemic load of approximately 14.5, which is moderate, so serving size should be measured carefully and sweetened toppings avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beta-glucan soluble fibre (15.4 g/100 g) with FDA-recognized LDL-cholesterol-lowering effect; zero cholesterol; low saturated fat (1.34 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oat bran is strongly cardioprotective: the FDA recognizes that 3 g/day of beta-glucan soluble fibre from oat bran may reduce the risk of coronary heart disease by lowering LDL cholesterol; with zero dietary cholesterol, low saturated fat (1.34 g/100 g), and high potassium (566 mg/100 g), oat bran is an evidence-based inclusion in a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome modulation of immune response; free of trans fat and excess saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Oat bran may benefit individuals with rheumatoid arthritis through its high dietary fibre content supporting gut microbiome diversity and immune modulation; patients on immunosuppressants with oat sensitivity or gluten cross-reactivity concerns should confirm use of certified gluten-free oat bran.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oat bran (uncooked) is the outer bran layer of the oat grain (Avena sativa), separated during milling, and consumed in its raw, dry form before any cooking or hydration. It appears as light brown, coarse flakes with a mildly nutty aroma and earthy flavour. When prepared as a hot cereal by cooking with water or milk, it becomes creamy and porridge-like with a slightly chewy texture. Nutritionally, oat bran is one of the most fibre-dense whole-grain ingredients available: it provides 15.4 g of dietary fibre per 100 g, a substantial proportion of which is beta-glucan soluble fibre — the specific fibre type recognised by the FDA and EFSA for its LDL-cholesterol-lowering and postprandial glucose-attenuating effects. It is also a notable plant-based protein source at 17.3 g/100 g, making it relevant for vegetarian and vegan dietary patterns. The energy density is 246 kcal/100 g, with carbohydrates at 66.22 g/100 g and fat at 7.03 g/100 g (predominantly unsaturated). Sodium is negligible at 4 mg/100 g, while potassium is high at 566 mg/100 g, supporting cardiovascular and blood pressure health. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low), which is considerably lower than refined oat products, owing to the viscous beta-glucan matrix that slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Oat bran is widely used in Western and global health-conscious diets as a breakfast porridge, added to smoothies, incorporated into baked goods (muffins, bread, pancakes), or mixed into yogurt. It is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and kosher dietary patterns. It is not inherently gluten-free due to potential cross-contamination with gluten-containing grains during processing. Clinically, oat bran is recommended for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia, and cardiovascular disease risk.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB database (confidence 0.95, reviewed status) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169722 (oat bran, raw), which is an exact match. Saturated fat and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA SR Legacy. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed publication. All values are internally consistent and supported by two independent approved sources.
notes: Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per both HPB and USDA SR Legacy — oat bran is a plant-derived food that naturally contains no dietary cholesterol (confirmed zero). Trans_fat_g is 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy; oat bran is a minimally processed plant food with no industrial trans fats (confirmed zero). Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as oat bran is a plant food containing no vitamin B12 (naturally zero). Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy (naturally zero in unfortified oat bran). Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy (naturally zero). Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy (naturally zero). Saturated fat (1.34 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (2.18 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.54 g/100 g), and additional micronutrients (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6, folate, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 169722 to supplement HPB primary values. Oat bran is not certified gluten-free by default; individuals with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity should use certified gluten-free oat bran. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI Database threshold (GI ≤55 = low).
